Nq7ok-OyEpg:SUMMARY:
- The video starts with an introduction to the concept of a link list, which is a data structure with a fixed size, stored in the Heap memory (at 1:33).
- A link list is similar to an array, but the elements are not contiguous in memory, and each element stores a pointer to the next element (at 4:35).
- The starting point of a link list is called the Head, which is stored at a specific location in memory (at 6:49).
- To traverse a link list, you start at the Head and follow the pointers to each element (at 8:38).
- In C++, a link list node is typically defined as a struct with two members: the data and a pointer to the next node (at 17:20).
- To initialize a link list node, a constructor is used, which sets the data and next pointer (at 18:03).
- In Java, a link list node is typically defined as a class with two members: the data and a reference to the next node (at 24:38).
- To traverse a link list, a temporary variable is used to keep track of the current node, and the next node is found using the next pointer (at 35:02).
- The time complexity of traversing a link list is O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the list (at 37:29).
- To find the length of a link list, a counter is used, and the counter is incremented for each node traversed (at 40:39).
- To delete a node from a link list, the previous node's next pointer is updated to skip the node to be deleted (at 42:48).
- The video ends with a preview of the next lecture, which will cover deletion and insertion in a link list.

9kdHxplyl5I:SUMMARY:
- The video discusses the "2 pointer and sliding window" playlist from the A2Z DS course. (at 0:03)
- The video starts with an example array with positive and negative numbers. (at 1:36)
- The first step in solving constant window problems is to assign a constant window size and move it while keeping a condition in check. (at 5:56)
- The second pattern is to find the longest subarray with sum less than or equal to K. (at 6:41)
- The Brute Force approach for this problem involves generating all possible subarrays and checking their sums. (at 8:29)
- The Brute Force approach has a time complexity of O(n^2) and a space complexity of O(1). (at 13:03)
- The typical template for solving this problem involves starting with a window size of 1, expanding the window to the right, and shrinking it to the left when the sum exceeds K. (at 13:47)
- The time complexity of this approach is O(n) and the space complexity is O(1). (at 24:10)
- The video discusses an optimization technique where instead of shrinking the window by one element at a time, you can keep the maximum length and only increase it when the current window is valid. (at 28:00)
- This optimization reduces the time complexity to O(n) and the space complexity remains O(1). (at 30:26)
nNXE-Bq3oG0:SUMMARY:
- The IT job market is seeing a lot of interesting changes, with companies hiring again and a report by Gartner stating that some companies have stopped using AI due to its limitations (at 0:00).

- A Chinese company had a 13-hour outage and loss of crores due to an AI outage in the China region, and similar incidents happened in February and March of 2026 (at 1:18).

- A major company in North America had a huge outage where 99% of orders vanished, resulting in a loss of 6.3 million orders, and the company could only solve 20-30% of the tasks (at 1:38).

- Real-world testing of AI systems is not comparable to the testing environment, which makes it difficult for companies to understand the limitations of AI (at 2:00).

- Companies are using AI as a shield to let employees go, and this is a cost-cutting measure to invest in AI infrastructure (at 3:29).

- Despite the market being down for entry-level engineers, there is increasing demand for mid-level engineers and experts in the market (at 4:57).

- The software industry as a whole is growing, and companies that don't use software will be coming online soon, creating new job opportunities (at 5:40).

- Large language models have a bottleneck and upper limit, and experts like Yann LeCun and the CEO of Anthropic have spoken about the limitations of these models (at 6:21).

- Understanding large language models and their 3D structure is key to grasping their limitations and potential (at 7:08).

- The speaker encourages viewers to study machine learning, AI, and data science, as it's a good time to enter the field and human intervention will always be required (at 8:11).
xwjS0iZhw4I:SUMMARY:
- The problem to be solved in this video is about balanced parentheses, which means checking if the order of opening and closing brackets in a string is correct (at 1:36).
- A string with balanced parentheses follows the order of opening and closing brackets, where each closing bracket has a corresponding opening bracket (at 1:56).
- There are three conditions to check if a string is balanced: every closing bracket has a corresponding opening bracket, every opening bracket has a corresponding closing bracket, and the order of brackets is correct (at 2:16).
- The correct approach to solve this problem is by using a stack data structure to preserve the opening brackets and check if they have corresponding closing brackets (at 3:23).
- When encountering an opening bracket, the last opening bracket encountered is preserved in the stack; when encountering a closing bracket, the top element of the stack is removed (at 4:44).
- If all brackets are matched correctly, the string is balanced, and if there are unmatched opening brackets, the string is unbalanced (at 6:12).
- A stack is used to preserve the opening brackets, and by iterating over the string, we can check if each bracket has a corresponding match (at 9:02).
- The time complexity of this approach is O(n), where n is the length of the string, and the space complexity is also O(n) because in the worst case, we may store all opening brackets in the stack (at 11:39).
